title: REST API
definition: An architectural style for building web APIs using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) with stateless communication. Resources are identified by URLs, and data is exchanged in JSON format.
why_used: The standard for exposing ML models as web services. Simple, stateless, universally supported, and easily consumed by any client (web app, mobile, other services).
interview_point: REST constraints: 1) Stateless — each request contains all info needed (no server-side sessions). 2) Uniform Interface — consistent URL patterns and HTTP methods. 3) Cacheable — responses indicate cacheability. HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE. Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error.
limitations: Multiple round trips for complex data graphs
real_world_example:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional
import logging

app = FastAPI(title='ML Service API', version='2.0.0')

class InferenceRequest(BaseModel):
    features: list[float] = Field(..., min_length=1, description='Input feature vector')
    model_version: Optional[str] = 'latest'

class InferenceResponse(BaseModel):
    prediction: int
    confidence: float
    model_version: str
    request_id: str

@app.post('/v1/predict', response_model=InferenceResponse, status_code=200)
async def predict(request: InferenceRequest):
    try:
        result = model.predict([request.features])[0]
        prob = model.predict_proba([request.features])[0].max()
        return InferenceResponse(
            prediction=int(result),
            confidence=round(float(prob), 4),
            model_version=model_version,
            request_id=generate_uuid()
        )
    except Exception as e:
        logging.error(f'Prediction failed: {e}')
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail='Model inference failed')
```

title: Microservices
definition: An architectural pattern where an application is broken into small, independently deployable services, each responsible for a specific business capability. Services communicate via APIs or message queues.
why_used: In ML systems, microservices separate concerns: data ingestion, feature engineering, model inference, and monitoring can be independently scaled, updated, and deployed.
interview_point: Microservices vs Monolith: Monolith is simpler to develop and debug but harder to scale. Microservices scale independently (scale inference without scaling training), enable polyglot (Python model service + Go API gateway), but introduce network complexity and operational overhead. Start monolithic, extract services when needed.
advantages: Fault isolation — one service failure doesn't crash all
limitations: Distributed system complexity (service discovery, load balancing)
use_cases: Separate ML model serving from training pipeline
real_world_example:
```yaml
# docker-compose.yml for ML microservices
version: '3.8'
services:
  api-gateway:
    build: ./gateway
    ports: ['80:80']
    depends_on: [inference-service, feature-service]
  
  feature-service:
    build: ./feature_engineering
    environment:
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379
    deploy:
      replicas: 2
  
  inference-service:
    build: ./model_serving
    environment:
      - MODEL_PATH=/models/prod_model.pkl
    volumes:
      - ./models:/models:ro
    deploy:
      replicas: 4  # Scale inference independently
    resources:
      reservations:
        devices:
          - driver: nvidia
            count: 1
            capabilities: [gpu]
  
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    # Feature cache for low-latency serving
```

title: Design Patterns
definition: Reusable solutions to commonly occurring problems in software design. Categorized as Creational (object creation), Structural (object composition), and Behavioral (object communication). From the Gang of Four book.
why_used: Understanding design patterns enables writing clean, maintainable, and extensible ML systems. Patterns like Strategy (swappable models), Factory (model registry), and Observer (monitoring) are fundamental to ML engineering.
interview_point: Key patterns in ML systems: 1) Strategy — swap different model implementations (sklearn, PyTorch) behind a common interface. 2) Factory — model registry that creates the right model class by name. 3) Repository — abstract data access layer. 4) Observer — trigger retraining when drift is detected. 5) Decorator — add logging/caching to model predictions.
advantages: Common vocabulary for system design discussions
